IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—detecting non-compositional and weakly compositional compound nouns—and demonstrates that combining traditional compositional (sparse, syntax-aware APT) methods with modern low-dimensional embeddings yields significant improvements, with the APT approach further improving results by preserving syntactic structure. Its finding that syntactic structure is crucial and that compositionality detection can both evaluate and guide compositional methods is likely to influence researchers in distributional semantics and composition. However, evaluation on a single 90-item dataset (Reddy et al., 2011) and methodological limitations (corpus preprocessing choices, sparse representation issues) limit immediate generality, so impact is strong but not transformative. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem—detecting non-compositional and weakly compositional compound nouns—and demonstrates that combining traditional compositional (APT) methods with modern low-dimensional embeddings yields significant improvements, with further gains when syntactic structure is preserved. The claim that compositionality detection can both evaluate and guide improvements to compositional models (e.g., smoothing phrasal representations) gives the work practical utility beyond mere evaluation. However, the empirical scope is limited (ukWaC preprocessing and a single 90-item Reddy et al. dataset), so while the contribution is likely to influence researchers and open follow-up work, it is not yet transformative across the broader NLP field.

Its finding that syntactic structure is crucial and that compositionality detection can both evaluate and guide compositional methods is likely to influence researchers in distributional semantics and composition. However, evaluation on a single 90-item dataset (Reddy et al., 2011) and methodological limitations (corpus preprocessing choices, sparse representation issues) limit immediate generality, so impact is strong but not transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are solid and compare the APT syntactically-aware composition method against neural embedding baselines and the REDDY baseline on the ukWaC corpus and the Reddy et al. 90-compound dataset, with hyperparameters reported and analyses (e.g. effect of shifted PMI, behavior of intersective vs. additive composition, varying h). The paper also includes sensible sensitivity discussion and failure/limitation notes (sparsity hurts intersective composition; preprocessing dropped internal modifier dependency paths; need to evaluate beyond 90 compounds). Related work is described as thorough. The main gap is limited evaluation breadth (only 90 compounds and a single phrase type), so additional datasets/phrase types would be needed for full generality. | B(4): The experiments are solid: they evaluate on a dependency-parsed ukWaC corpus with the Reddy et al. 90-compound dataset, compare APT-based aligned compositions (⊔INT / ⊔UNI) to Reddy's baseline and neural embedding compositions, and report hyperparameters. The paper includes sensitivity/negative-result analyses (e.g. shifted PMI harming performance, sparsity issues impacting intersective composition) and discusses preprocessing and dataset limitations. Related work coverage is reported as thorough. Reproducibility is reasonably supported by reported hyperparameters and methodological detail, but lack of released code and some preprocessing choices (compound recombination, ignored dependency paths) leave minor gaps for exact replication.

The paper also includes sensible sensitivity discussion and failure/limitation notes (sparsity hurts intersective composition; preprocessing dropped internal modifier dependency paths; need to evaluate beyond 90 compounds). CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized—methods (preprocessing, APT construction, composition operators) and experiments (datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics) are presented in a logical sequence—and the main contributions are stated early (combining embeddings with compositional methods, the APT approach). Notation and experimental details seem consistent and sufficiently reported (hyperparameters given, specific performance figures such as 0.74 for cbow are shown), and related work is covered thoroughly. Writing quality is described as good and limitations are explicitly acknowledged, so only minor presentation refinements seem necessary. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro, clear methodology with preprocessing, composition and evaluation, experiments on ukWaC and Reddy et al., and conclusion) and states its main contributions early and explicitly (combining embeddings with compositional methods, importance of syntactic structure, and the APT approach). Notation and methods are presented with specific operators and hyperparameters (e.g. ⊔INT, ⊔UNI, weighting h) and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work coverage. One minor presentation caveat is that the summary does not mention the quality or presence of figures/tables, so there may be small gaps in visual presentation or illustrative material.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Most essential details are provided: datasets (ukWaC and Reddy et al.’s 90-compound dataset) and evaluation metric (correlation with human judgments) are specified, and hyperparameters and composition operations (⊔INT, ⊔UNI with weighting h) are reported. The preprocessing pipeline is described at a high level (dependency-parsed ukWaC with 90 compounds recombined and a noted decision to ignore certain dependency paths), but implementation-level details and scripts are not provided. Code is not mentioned, so full reimplementation would require effort to reproduce exact APT construction and alignment decisions despite the reported parameters. Overall, reproducible with minor missing implementation details and no released code. | B(4): Most key elements needed for replication are reported: hyperparameters and model configurations are stated, the preprocessing step (dependency-parsed ukWaC with 90 target compounds recombined) and composition operators (⊔INT, ⊔UNI with weighting h) are described, and evaluation uses correlation with the Reddy et al. human judgments. The datasets themselves are standard/public (ukWaC and Reddy et al.), but the paper does not mention released code and some preprocessing/implementation specifics (exact APT construction/alignment details and handling of shifted PMI/negative sampling) are only partially described. Therefore the work is mostly reproducible but would require contacting authors or reimplementing non-trivial components from the descriptions.

reviewer_a.justification: Most essential details are provided: datasets (ukWaC and Reddy et al.’s 90-compound dataset) and evaluation metric (correlation with human judgments) are specified, and hyperparameters and composition operations (⊔INT, ⊔UNI with weighting h) are reported. The preprocessing pipeline is described at a high level (dependency-parsed ukWaC with 90 compounds recombined and a noted decision to ignore certain dependency paths), but implementation-level details and scripts are not provided. Code is not mentioned, so full reimplementation would require effort to reproduce exact APT construction and alignment decisions despite the reported parameters. Therefore the work is mostly reproducible but would require contacting authors or reimplementing non-trivial components from the descriptions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—compositionality detection and distributional semantics—using both classic (APT typed sparse representations) and modern (word2vec) techniques, which is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. Its problem formulation and audience are appropriate: it uses a dependency-parsed ukWaC corpus, evaluates on the Reddy et al. (2011) human-judged dataset of 90 compound nouns, and reports correlation with human judgments while comparing against relevant baselines (Reddy baseline and neural embedding compositions). The methods and evaluation follow community standards, the related work coverage is thorough, and stated limitations (dataset scope, sparse representation issues) are acknowledged, so the paper is well positioned for a top venue. | B(5): The paper addresses compositionality detection and distributional semantics—core topics for top NLP/ML venues—and proposes methods (APT sparse representations combined with word embeddings) that are directly relevant to that community. It uses appropriate resources and evaluation standards (dependency-parsed ukWaC, the Reddy et al. 2011 dataset of 90 compounds, correlation with human judgments) and compares against established baselines (Reddy baseline, word2vec compositions). Although the authors acknowledge limitations (small target set of 90 compounds and corpus/preprocessing choices), the problem formulation, methods, and positioning are well-aligned with a top-tier NLP/ML audience.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against relevant baselines (Reddy et al. 2011 weighted-addition baseline and ad-hoc word2vec composition) using the same Reddy et al. dataset and correlation-with-human-judgments metric, and reports hyperparameters and results (e.g. 0.74 for cbow). It clearly analyzes differences between its APT/syntactic approach and embedding-only composition (different composition operators, sparsity/intersection issues) and acknowledges limitations and the need for more datasets. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the summary does not mention comparisons to a broader set of recent neural compositional models or additional datasets, so a minor set of contemporary baselines appears omitted. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Reddy et al. 2011 weighted-addition baseline and ad-hoc neural embedding compositions using word2vec) and evaluates under the same conditions (the Reddy et al. dataset of 90 compounds, ukWaC-derived representations, and correlation with human judgments; hyperparameters are reported). Differences between their APT approach and embedding baselines are analyzed, and limitations that could affect fairness (e.g. shifted PMI effects, sparse representations, and the single-dataset/phrase-type focus) are explicitly acknowledged. The main shortcoming is scope: comparisons are strong but limited to one dataset/type of phrase, so not fully comprehensive across other datasets and phrase types.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against relevant baselines (Reddy et al. 2011 weighted-addition baseline and ad-hoc word2vec composition) using the same Reddy et al. dataset and correlation-with-human-judgments metric, and reports hyperparameters and results (e.g. 0.74 for cbow). It clearly analyzes differences between its APT/syntactic approach and embedding-only composition (different composition operators, sparsity/intersection issues) and acknowledges limitations and the need for more datasets. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the summary does not mention comparisons to a broader set of recent neural compositional models or additional datasets, so a minor set of contemporary baselines appears omitted.

Differences between their APT approach and embedding baselines are analyzed, and limitations that could affect fairness (e.g. shifted PMI effects, sparse representations, and the single-dataset/phrase-type focus) are explicitly acknowledged. The main shortcoming is scope: comparisons are strong but limited to one dataset/type of phrase, so not fully comprehensive across other datasets and phrase types.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a notable novelty by integrating traditional typed sparse (APT) compositional representations with low-dimensional neural embeddings and demonstrating improved compositionality detection versus ad-hoc embedding composition (e.g., cbow/skip-gram). Its emphasis on maintaining syntactic structure within contextual features (the APT-based integrated compositional distributional approach) and the empirical claim that this yields further gains differentiates it clearly from prior work like Reddy et al. (2011). Framing compositionality detection both as an evaluation tool for compositional methods and as a way to guide smoothing/adjustment of phrasal representations is a useful perspective that would inform experts, so the contribution is more than a simple recombination of known techniques. | B(3): The paper presents a non-trivial, incremental contribution by combining established elements — traditional compositional distributional methods (APT typed sparse representations and intersection/uniform composition operators) with modern low-dimensional word embeddings — and showing empirical gains on the Reddy et al. (2011) 90-compound dataset. Its emphasis on maintaining syntactic structure via the APT approach and the framing of compositionality detection as both an evaluation and a method-improvement tool add useful perspective, but these build on prior techniques rather than introducing a wholly new paradigm. Given the small, specific dataset and use of known composition operators, the work is notable and informative to experts but not highly novel.

Framing compositionality detection both as an evaluation tool for compositional methods and as a way to guide smoothing/adjustment of phrasal representations is a useful perspective that would inform experts, so the contribution is more than a simple recombination of known techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by experimental evidence: the paper compares APT-based compositional methods, neural embedding baselines (word2vec cbow/skip-gram), and the REDDY baseline on the Reddy et al. (2011) 90-compound dataset and reports improved correlations (e.g. 0.74 for cbow), and hyperparameters are reported. No mathematical derivations are indicated in the summary, and nothing suggests incorrectness there. Methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and a realistic corpus (ukWaC), but there are limitations that weaken generality and interpretation (small 90-item evaluation set, preprocessing choice that drops dependency paths involving modifiers, and no mention of statistical significance testing). Given these minor but important caveats, the technical claims are credible though further validation is needed to fully generalize the conclusions. | B(4): The paper's principal claims are supported by empirical comparisons on the Reddy et al. 90-compound dataset using ukWaC, showing improved correlations (e.g. cbow 0.74) when combining embeddings with APT compositional methods and against the Reddy baseline and ad‑hoc embedding compositions. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the reported methodology uses appropriate baselines and hyperparameter reporting; however the evaluation is limited to a small (90-item) dataset and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing. The authors acknowledge key limitations (PMI effects, sparsity issues, preprocessing choices), so while the experimental evidence supports the conclusions, broader validation would be needed to fully generalize the claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are supported by experimental evidence: the paper compares APT-based compositional methods, neural embedding baselines (word2vec cbow/skip-gram), and the REDDY baseline on the Reddy et al. (2011) 90-compound dataset and reports improved correlations (e.g. 0.74 for cbow), and hyperparameters are reported. No mathematical derivations are indicated in the summary, and nothing suggests incorrectness there. Methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and a realistic corpus (ukWaC), but there are limitations that weaken generality and interpretation (small 90-item evaluation set, preprocessing choice that drops dependency paths involving modifiers, and no mention of statistical significance testing). Given these minor but important caveats, the technical claims are credible though further validation is needed to fully generalize the conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's principal claims are supported by empirical comparisons on the Reddy et al. 90-compound dataset using ukWaC, showing improved correlations (e.g. cbow 0.74) when combining embeddings with APT compositional methods and against the Reddy baseline and ad‑hoc embedding compositions. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the reported methodology uses appropriate baselines and hyperparameter reporting; however the evaluation is limited to a small (90-item) dataset and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing. The authors acknowledge key limitations (PMI effects, sparsity issues, preprocessing choices), so while the experimental evidence supports the conclusions, broader validation would be needed to fully generalize the claims.

main_claims: Combining traditional compositional methods with state-of-the-art low-dimensional word representations (word embeddings) significantly improves compositionality detection results.
An integrated compositional distributional approach that maintains syntactic structure within contextual features (the APT approach) yields further improvements over ad-hoc composition of embeddings.
Knowledge of syntactic structure is crucial for fine-grained understanding and for improving detection of non-compositional (and weakly compositional) compound nouns.

Compositionality detection can be used both to evaluate compositional methods without confounding similarity judgements and to guide improvements in compositional methods (e.g., by smoothing observed phrasal representations when phrases are non-compositional).
_summary.methodology: They preprocess a fully dependency-parsed ukWaC corpus to recombine 90 target compound nouns into single lexical items, build elementary APT (typed sparse) representations and neural embeddings (word2vec), align constituent APTs and compose them using ⊔INT (pointwise min) or ⊔UNI (pointwise addition, with weighting parameter h), and compare composed representations against observed phrasal vectors and human compositionality judgments.
datasets: Reddy et al. (2011) dataset of 90 compound nouns with human compositionality judgments
baselines: REDDY baseline (Reddy et al., 2011: weighted addition of untyped co-occurrence vectors)
Neural embeddings composed ad-hoc (word2vec cbow/skip-gram models)
metrics: Correlation with human judgments (reported performance value e.g. 0.74 for cbow with 100 dimensions and subsampling t=10)
limitations: Further work needed with other datasets and other types of phrase (stated need to evaluate beyond the 90 compound nouns).
Shifted PMI (analogous to negative sampling) had a strong negative effect in this study, likely due to low frequency of many compound nouns.
Sparse nature of elementary representations can hurt intersective composition: if composition fails to recover highly informative but rare contexts, similarity is heavily impacted.

During preprocessing, dependency paths including the modifying constituent were ignored, which the authors justify but is a methodological limitation (modifications of internal parts of the phrase are not applied to the phrase).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - compositionality detection / distributional semantics (compound nouns)
